title: Cambridge Health Group to invest $26.64m in Jeddah hospital rehab wing - Arabian Business: Latest News on
summary: Cambridge Health Group will invest SAR 100 million in Jeddah to expand hospital capacity to 270 beds and add rehabilitation surgery services
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
Cambridge Health Group will invest about SAR 100 million ($26.64 million) to expand Cambridge Hospital Jeddah, lifting capacity from 200 beds to about 270 and creating Jeddah’s first dedicated rehabilitation hospital.

The project adds as many as 70 beds, three rehabilitation gyms with robotic technology and two operating theatres for musculoskeletal and spinal procedures. Completion is targeted for early 2028.

Cambridge is launching the project after the Jeddah site completed an earlier growth phase that doubled capacity from 100 beds to 200 in 2025. Amanat disclosed in March that the facility was operating near full capacity.

And strengthening the case for another expansion cycle in Saudi Arabia’s Western Region. Cambridge has also linked the buildout to rising demand for high acuity and specialised rehabilitation services.

Surgery and rehabilitation under one roof

Jeddah scheme widens Cambridge’s service mix beyond a bed increase. Planned theatres will let the group connect surgical intervention with inpatient recovery and outpatient rehabilitation within one care pathway. Robotic gyms will support more intensive therapy programmes for resident patients and day cases.

Service mix is set to cover both inpatient and outpatient programmes, allowing longer case management and steadier utilisation across therapy units. Outpatient capability also gives the hospital a larger stream of follow-up work after discharge.

Dr. Ali Saeed bin Harmal Aldhaheri, Chairman of Cambridge Health Group, said the investment fits a long-term plan to extend the group’s lead in post-acute care and rehabilitation across the GCC.

He added that Saudi Arabia remains a strategic market for Cambridge and described the Jeddah project as a stronger specialist offering that spans surgery, rehabilitation and recovery.

Capacity target moves closer to 1,000 beds

Completion will lift Cambridge Health Group’s total GCC capacity to about 800 beds, advancing a mid-term plan to reach 1,000 beds. Amanat’s 2025 earnings presentation showed Cambridge ended last year with 715 total beds and 625 licensed and operational beds, with further licensing underway during 2026.

Separate company guidance issued in March placed expected operational capacity for 2026 at about 725 beds. March guidance also pointed to future service lines, including haemodialysis and sports performance services.

Amanat’s latest disclosures also show why management is pressing ahead. Cambridge generated a record 2025 revenue of AED 404 million, up 11 per cent year on year, while EBITDA rose 14 per cent to AED 100 million. The group operates six facilities across the UAE and Saudi Arabia and employs more than 1,200 healthcare professionals.

Network includes two hospitals in Abu Dhabi and one in Al Ain alongside Saudi assets in Jeddah, Khobar and Dhahran. March disclosures also confirmed full ownership of Sukoon, which houses the Jeddah asset.

Amanat funds another growth step in healthcare

Wael Abdallah, CEO of Cambridge Health Group, said the project addresses clear demand for specialised post-acute and rehabilitative care in Saudi Arabia. He noted that surgical capability, inpatient care and outpatient rehabilitation will sit inside one operating network in Jeddah.

Amanat reported AED 931.7 million in continuing operations revenue for 2025, alongside EBITDA of AED 380.4 million and cash and bank balances of AED 1.45 billion. Balance sheet strength gives the listed group room to keep adding beds and specialist services across its healthcare business.
